summary: Drew DeVault follows up on his earlier scathing review of RaptorCS's POWER9 Blackbird PC, reporting that after his article frontpaged on Hacker News, Raptor reached out, diagnosed multiple hardware defects, and replaced both the CPU and mainboard. He now has a working machine and is impressed with its performance. However, he refused to publish a positive follow-up until Raptor also fixed their systemic support problems for all customers, which they did by implementing a self-service RMA system and other improvements. He details the spectacular failures of the original board, which a Raptor staff member called the most broken board they'd ever seen.
key_points: Raptor replaced both the defective CPU and mainboard, and the new hardware installed painlessly
DeVault refused to give credit until Raptor fixed support for all customers, not just those with viral blog posts, prompting systemic changes including self-service RMA
The original board had an extraordinary cascade of failures including broken BMC ethernet, boot loops, and ncurses output being piped into a shell
The working machine performs well with 32-thread kernel compilation and KVM support, and will serve as a build slave for SourceHut's ppc64le builds
money_quotes: I refuse to give any company credit for waking up their support team only when a scathing article about them frontpages on Hacker News.
I told them I wouldn't publish a positive follow-up unless they also convinced me that the support experience had been fixed for the typical user as well.
petitboot's ncurses output was being piped into a shell and executed (what the fuck?)
In the immortal words of James Mickens, 'I HAVE NO TOOLS BECAUSE I HAVE DESTROYED MY TOOLS WITH MY TOOLS.'
This is easily the most broken board I've seen, ever, and that includes prototypes.
This machine is so badly broken that it would actually be hilarious if the manufacturer had been more present in the troubleshooting process.
They did give me a little heart attack, however, by sending the replacement CPU to me in the same box I had returned the faulty CPU back to them with - a box which I had labelled 'BAD CPU'.
key_insight: Companies deserve credit not for fixing problems when publicly shamed, but for implementing systemic changes that prevent all customers from suffering the same failures.
